title: Per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels
caption:
Per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels, 2025
Electricity generation from coal, oil, and gas, measured in kilowatt-hours per person.

The choropleth map displays global variation in per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels for 2025, using a continuous color scale ranging from 0 to 10,000 kilowatt-hours per person. The highest values, represented by the darkest brown shades, appear in the Middle East, specifically Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, where generation exceeds 5,000 kWh per person. High levels of generation, falling between 2,000 and 5,000 kWh, are visible across North America, including the United States and Canada, as well as in Australia, Russia, and parts of East Asia like South Korea and Japan. Western European nations such as Germany and France occupy the middle of the scale, generally falling within the 1,000 to 2,000 kWh band. Moving toward the lower end of the spectrum, countries in South America like Brazil and Argentina, along with China and India, show values typically between 200 and 1,000 kWh per person. The lightest shades, indicating generation below 200 kWh, cover most of sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia. Areas marked with diagonal hatching, such as Greenland and parts of central Africa, indicate regions where no data is available. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of high per capita fossil fuel electricity generation in wealthy industrialized nations and major oil-producing states, while the lowest values correspond to developing economies in the Global South.
